en.extract:
The hooded mountain tanager (Buthraupis montana) is a species of bird in the tanager family Thraupidae. It is the only member of the genus Buthraupis. It is found in the forests and woodlands of the Andean highlands of Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Venezuela, at altitudes between 1,800 and 3,000 m (5,900 and 9,800 ft). At 23 centimetres (9.1 in) and 96 grams (3.4 oz), it is one of the largest tanagers. It has a black head and thighs, a blue back and bright yellow belly, with red eyes.


Taxonomy

The hooded mountain tanager was formally described in 1837 by the French naturalists Alcide d'Orbigny and Frédéric de Lafresnaye from a specimen collected in the Bolivian Yungas. They coined the binomial name Aglaia montana. It is now the only species placed in the genus Buthraupis that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek bou- meaning "huge" and thraupis, an unknown small bird but used by ornithologists to signify a tanager. The specific name is from the Latin montanus meaning "of the mountains".
Six subspecies are recognised:

Buthraupis montana venezuelana Aveledo & Perez, 1989 – northwest Venezuela
Buthraupis montana gigas (Bonaparte, 1851) – north Colombia
Buthraupis montana cucullata (Jardine & Selby, 1842) – west, central Colombia to Ecuador
Buthraupis montana cyanonota Berlepsch & Stolzmann, 1896 – north, central Peru
Buthraupis montana saturata Berlepsch & Stolzmann, 1906 – southeast Peru
Buthraupis montana montana (d'Orbigny & Lafresnaye, 1837) – west Bolivia

The '''hooded mountain tanager''' ('''''Buthraupis montana''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the tanager family [[Thraupidae]]. It is the only member of the [[genus]] '''''Buthraupis'''''. It is found in the forests and woodlands of the [[Andean]] highlands of [[Bolivia]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[Peru]], and [[Venezuela]], at altitudes between {{convert|1800 and 3000|m||abbr=on}}.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021"/><ref name="Princeton758">{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ErumDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA758 |pages=758–759 |title=Birds of Venezuela |author=Steven L. Hilty |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2003 |isbn=0-691-09250-8 }}</ref> At {{convert|23|cm|in}} and {{convert|96|g|oz}}, it is one of the largest tanagers. It has a black head and thighs, a blue back and bright yellow belly, with red eyes.<ref name="Princeton758"/>

==Taxonomy==
[[File:Buthraupis montana Hooded Mountain Tanager, Puno, Peru (cropped).jpg|thumb|left|In Peru]]

The hooded mountain tanager was [[species description|formally described]] in 1837 by the French naturalists [[Alcide d'Orbigny]] and [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye]] from a specimen collected in the [[Bolivian Yungas]]. They coined the [[binomial name]] ''Aglaia montana''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=d'Orbigny | first1=Alcide | author1-link=Alcide d'Orbigny | last2=Lafresnaye | first2=Frédéric de | author2-link=Frédéric de Lafresnaye | year=1837 | title=Synopsis avium | language=Latin | journal=Magasin de Zoologie | volume=7 | issue=2 | pages=1–88 [32] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37087477 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1970 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=13 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=329 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483564}}</ref> It is now the only species placed in the genus ''Buthraupis'' that was introduced in 1851 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Cabanis | first=Jean | author-link=Jean Cabanis | year=1850–1851 | title=Museum Heineanum: Verzeichniss der ornithologischen Sammlung des Oberamtmann Ferdinand Heine, auf Gut St. Burchard vor Halberstadt | volume=1 | language=German, Latin | place=Halberstadt | publisher=R. Frantz | page=29 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49584408 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=July 2020 | title=Tanagers and allies | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/tanagers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 November 2020 }}</ref> The genus name combines the [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc-Latn|bou-}} meaning "huge" and {{Lang|grc-Latn|thraupis}}, an unknown small bird but used by ornithologists to signify a tanager. The specific name is from the [[Latin]] {{Lang|la|montanus}} meaning "of the mountains".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=81, 259 }}</ref>

Six [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>

* ''Buthraupis montana venezuelana'' Aveledo & Perez, 1989 – northwest Venezuela
* ''Buthraupis montana gigas'' ([[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1851) – north Colombia
* ''Buthraupis montana cucullata'' ([[Sir William Jardine, 7th Baronet|Jardine]] & [[Prideaux John Selby|Selby]], 1842) – west, central Colombia to Ecuador
* ''Buthraupis montana cyanonota'' [[Hans von Berlepsch|Berlepsch]] & [[Jan Sztolcman|Stolzmann]], 1896 – north, central Peru
* ''Buthraupis montana saturata'' Berlepsch & Stolzmann, 1906 – southeast Peru
* ''Buthraupis montana montana'' (d'Orbigny & Lafresnaye, 1837) – west Bolivia
